topic: Geographic Themes (Three from the SL list)
subject: geography

items.q: Why is the global energy transition slower than many expect?
items.correct: Existing fossil-fuel infrastructure is sunk cost, low-carbon alternatives need vast capital, intermittency requires storage, and political/economic interests resist change — even as costs of renewables fall.
items.misconception: The energy transition is slow because renewable energy doesn't actually work.
items.random_wrong: The transition is slow because people don't want to give up cars.
items.why: Renewables are now often cheaper than fossil fuels per unit. The barriers are infrastructure, finance, grid integration and politics — a system inertia problem, not a technology limitation.

items.q: How is the dependency ratio calculated?
items.correct: (Population aged 0-14 plus population aged 65+) divided by population aged 15-64, multiplied by 100 — comparing dependants to the working-age population.
items.misconception: The dependency ratio is the number of children per family.
items.random_wrong: The dependency ratio is the number of unemployed people in a country.
items.why: It compares life-stage groups, not employment status. High ratios can come from many children (LICs) OR many elderly (HICs) — both create burden but very different policy challenges.

items.correct: Development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs — balancing economic, social and environmental goals.
items.misconception: Sustainable development means stopping all economic growth.
items.random_wrong: Sustainable development means using only solar power.
items.why: Students treat sustainability as anti-development. The Brundtland definition is about balance and intergenerational equity — not zero growth or one specific technology.
Correct answer: Crutzen and Stoermer. “Lovelock and Margulis” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many planetary boundaries did Rockstrom et al identify in 2009?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 9. “12” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Roughly what percentage of global internet traffic uses submarine cables?
items.why: Exact answer: 99 percent. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
